Full SOS builds bloom filter indexes for every eligible column in the table, which is more expensive in both storage overhead and the background build credits consumed. By specifying only customer_id and order_status — the columns with actual equality predicate lookups in production queries — the architect achieves the performance benefit for those queries at a fraction of the full-table SOS cost. This is particularly impactful on wide tables with many columns.

**A** is partially true but misses the cost efficiency angle.

**C** is incorrect — there is no 1,000-column limit; wide table SOS is an architectural choice, not a hard constraint.

**D** is incorrect — SOS uses bloom filters and access path structures layered on top of Snowflake's micro-partition architecture; there is no B-tree in Snowflake's storage layer.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: An architect is evaluating when NOT to use a clustering key. A table has 500,000 rows, data is loaded once per day, and queries frequently use full table scans (SELECT * with no WHERE clause) for reporting. Which statement correctly identifies why clustering is inappropriate for this table?

options.text: Clustering is inappropriate because 500,000 rows fit in very few micro-partitions (approximately 2-5 micro-partitions), so partition pruning provides no benefit — all partitions would be scanned anyway
Clustering is inappropriate because full table scans bypass the clustering metadata, making the clustering key invisible to the query planner
Clustering is inappropriate for tables with fewer than 50 million rows because the overhead exceeds the pruning benefit
Clustering should not be used on tables with daily loads because COPY INTO invalidates clustering metadata on every load

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because clustering delivers value through micro-partition pruning — skipping partitions that do not contain values matching the query predicate. A 500,000-row table (assuming ~500K rows / ~100K rows per micro-partition) occupies roughly 5 micro-partitions. With only 5 micro-partitions, even without clustering, a query with a selective predicate would scan at most a few partitions. More importantly, the stated query pattern is full table scans with no WHERE clause — pruning cannot help here regardless of clustering. Clustering has a cost (Automatic Clustering credits for maintenance), and on a small, scan-everything table, that cost yields no performance return.

**B** is incorrect — clustering metadata is always visible to the planner; full scans simply don't benefit from it.

**C** is a reasonable heuristic but not a hard rule.

**D** is incorrect — COPY INTO does not invalidate clustering metadata.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: An architect examines SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('SALES.PUBLIC.ORDERS', '(ORDER_DATE)') output and sees: average_depth = 3.8, total_partition_count = 12000, average_overlaps = 2.1. What does average_depth = 3.8 specifically indicate about the clustering state?

options.text: On average, a value in the ORDER_DATE column appears in 3.8 micro-partitions; a perfectly clustered table would have average_depth = 1.0, meaning each value resides in exactly one partition
The clustering key spans 3.8 depth levels in a B-tree index; values above 4.0 indicate index fragmentation
The table has 3.8 times more micro-partitions than the optimal number; this indicates over-partitioning
On average, 3.8% of micro-partitions are read during a typical ORDER_DATE range scan; values above 5.0% indicate poor clustering

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because average_depth in SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION measures the average number of micro-partitions that contain any given value of the clustering key. An average_depth of 1.0 would mean perfect clustering — every distinct ORDER_DATE value resides in exactly one set of micro-partitions with no overlap. An average_depth of 3.8 means that on average, a query filtering for a specific ORDER_DATE must read data from 3.8 micro-partitions. The closer to 1.0, the better the clustering. For a 12,000-partition table, 3.8 is reasonable but indicates some degradation from DML activity.

**B** is incorrect — Snowflake does not use B-tree indexes. C misinterprets average_depth as a partition count ratio. D misinterprets the metric as a percentage.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: A company is piloting Snowflake Hybrid Tables for a new application that requires row-level CRUD operations with millisecond latency. The architect needs to communicate the key architectural distinction between Hybrid Tables and standard Snowflake tables. Which statement is most accurate?

options.text: Hybrid Tables use the same columnar micro-partition storage as standard tables but add a row-level lock manager for transactional consistency

Hybrid Tables use a row-store (HybridDB/Unistore) architecture optimized for point-lookup and row-level DML operations, enabling OLTP-style workloads within Snowflake alongside analytical queries on standard columnar tables
Hybrid Tables are columnar tables with an additional secondary index layer; they support the same analytical query patterns as standard tables plus faster point lookups
Hybrid Tables automatically switch between row and columnar storage based on query type, making them universally faster for all workloads

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake Hybrid Tables (part of the Unistore architecture) use a row-oriented storage engine underneath, designed for transactional OLTP patterns: single-row inserts, updates, deletes, and point-lookups with millisecond response times. This is architecturally different from Snowflake's standard columnar micro-partition storage optimized for analytical scans. Hybrid Tables enable a single Snowflake account to run both OLTP-style applications and OLAP analytics without data movement between systems.

**A** is incorrect — Hybrid Tables do not use micro-partition storage with a lock manager added; they use fundamentally different row storage. C mischaracterizes Hybrid Tables as columnar with an index layer.

**D** is incorrect — Hybrid Tables are not universally faster; they are optimized for OLTP patterns, while standard tables remain superior for large analytical scans.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: An architect notices that queries run very fast immediately after data is loaded onto a warm warehouse, but the first query after the warehouse resumes from auto-suspend is noticeably slower, even when the underlying data has not changed. Why does the first post-resume query not benefit from the result cache even for an identical query?

options.text: The result cache is stored on the warehouse's local SSD; when the warehouse suspends, the SSD data is cleared, requiring a fresh scan on resume

The result cache is a global service-layer cache that persists across warehouse suspension; the first post-resume query DOES hit the result cache if the data has not changed and the SQL text matches exactly

The warehouse cache (local disk cache of micro-partitions) is cleared on suspension; the first query must re-read micro-partitions from remote cloud storage rather than from local SSD cache, causing the slowdown
Auto-suspend invalidates all result cache entries for that warehouse's recent queries as a security precaution

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct and addresses a subtle but important distinction: there are TWO caches in Snowflake. The result cache (global, service-layer) stores exact query results for 24 hours and IS available immediately after warehouse resume — identical queries against unchanged data DO hit the result cache. The LOCAL DISK CACHE (warehouse-level, SSD) stores decompressed micro-partitions from recent scans on the warehouse nodes' local storage. When the warehouse suspends, the local nodes are released and their SSD cache is lost. On resume, the first scan of any micro-partition must re-read from cloud storage, which is slower than SSD. The questioner's scenario — fast after loading (warm SSD cache), slow after resume (cold SSD cache) — perfectly describes local disk cache behavior, not result cache behavior. B correctly describes the result cache but doesn't explain the observed slowdown. A incorrectly attributes the result cache to warehouse SSD.

[Using Persisted Query Results](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/querying-persisted-results)

questions.stem: A company is evaluating Iceberg Tables in Snowflake. Select TWO scenarios where Iceberg Tables are the preferred choice over standard Snowflake-native tables. (Select TWO)

options.text: The data must be accessible by both Snowflake and Apache Spark/Databricks simultaneously without data duplication or ETL between systems
The team requires maximum query performance on analytical workloads where all access is exclusively through Snowflake
The organization has a policy requiring all analytical data to reside in open-format files on their own cloud storage (e.g., S3), maintaining full data portability and avoiding vendor lock-in
The workload involves heavy DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) operations at sub-second latency
The team wants to leverage Snowflake's Automatic Clustering and Search Optimization Service on the dataset for maximum query acceleration

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Iceberg Tables use the open Apache Iceberg table format on the customer's cloud storage, making the same data accessible to multiple engines — Snowflake can query it via Snowflake's Iceberg integration while Spark/Databricks can query the same files using their Iceberg connectors. This eliminates data duplication across platforms.

**C** is correct because Iceberg Tables store data in Parquet files in the customer's own object storage (S3, ADLS, GCS) — the customer owns and controls the files. If they leave Snowflake, the data remains in their storage in an open format. This satisfies data portability and anti-lock-in policies.

**B** is incorrect — for Snowflake-exclusive workloads, native Snowflake tables outperform Iceberg Tables because Snowflake can apply more aggressive optimization to its proprietary format.

**D** is incorrect — Iceberg Tables are optimized for analytical reads, not high-frequency OLTP DML.

**E** is incorrect — some Snowflake optimizations like full Automatic Clustering have limitations with Iceberg Tables.

[Search optimization service](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/search-optimization-service)

questions.stem: An operations engineer runs SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION('mydb.public.my_s3_stage') and receives an error indicating that the connection cannot be established. The stage uses a storage integration. Which troubleshooting sequence is most appropriate?

options.text: Re-create the external stage with new credentials because SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION errors always indicate expired access keys

Verify that the storage integration's IAM role trust policy in AWS includes the correct Snowflake account ARN and external ID; check that the S3 bucket policy allows the IAM role; confirm the storage integration's STORAGE_AWS_ROLE_ARN and STORAGE_ALLOWED_LOCATIONS match the stage URL
The error indicates a network policy is blocking the external stage connection; update the ALLOWED_IP_LIST to include the S3 endpoint IP ranges
Contact Snowflake Support immediately — SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION errors are always caused by Snowflake infrastructure issues, not customer configuration

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION is specifically designed to test connectivity between a Snowflake external stage and the underlying cloud storage. When it fails with a storage integration, the most common causes are IAM trust relationship issues — specifically, the AWS IAM role trust policy must explicitly trust Snowflake's AWS account ARN AND include the correct external ID (which Snowflake generates and displays in the storage integration). Additionally, the S3 bucket policy must allow the assumed IAM role to access it, and the STORAGE_ALLOWED_LOCATIONS in the integration must match the stage's URL.

**A** is incorrect — storage integrations use IAM role assumption, not access keys; there are no credentials to expire in the traditional sense.

**C** is incorrect — network policies govern Snowflake account connections, not outbound connectivity to S3.

**D** is incorrect — the vast majority of SYSTEM$VERIFY_EXTERNAL_STAGE_CONNECTION failures are configuration errors, not Snowflake infrastructure problems.

[Controlling network traffic with network policies](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/network-policies)

questions.stem: A development team wants to create an external function that calls a REST API to enrich customer data during a Snowflake query. They create an API Integration object. Later, the team also deploys a containerized service using Snowpark Container Services. Which statement correctly distinguishes the use of CREATE API INTEGRATION between these two scenarios?

options.text: CREATE API INTEGRATION is only used for external functions; Snowpark Container Services uses CREATE SERVICE INTEGRATION instead

For external functions, CREATE API INTEGRATION defines the API gateway endpoint and authentication method. For Snowpark Container Services, CREATE API INTEGRATION is also used but points to a different endpoint type (the Container Registry or internal Snowflake endpoint rather than an external gateway)
CREATE API INTEGRATION for external functions requires HTTPS endpoints on the public internet; Snowpark Container Services bypasses API Integration and uses internal Snowflake networking

Both external functions and Snowpark Container Services use identical API Integration configurations; the difference is only in how the integration is referenced in CREATE EXTERNAL FUNCTION vs CREATE SERVICE

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because CREATE API INTEGRATION is used in both scenarios but with different configurations. For external functions, the API Integration specifies an external API gateway (AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management, GCP API Gateway) as the ALLOWED_PREFIXES endpoint, enabling Snowflake to authenticate to and call that external REST endpoint. For Snowpark Container Services, a separate type of API Integration references Snowflake's internal image registry or external registry endpoints. The integration concept is shared but the API_PROVIDER and endpoint configuration differ between the two use cases.

**A** is incorrect — API Integration is not exclusive to external functions.

**C** is incorrect — while external functions commonly use public HTTPS endpoints, the API Integration mechanism itself is not constrained to public internet endpoints.

**D** is incorrect — the configurations are meaningfully different between the two use cases in terms of the API_PROVIDER and endpoint specifications.

[Snowpark Developer Guide for Python](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: An architect is designing an External OAuth integration with a custom identity provider. The security team asks about the EXTERNAL_OAUTH_SNOWFLAKE_USER_MAPPING_ATTRIBUTE parameter. What does this parameter control, and why does its value matter for security?

options.text: It specifies the OAuth scope claim name; the value must match a Snowflake role name for the authentication to succeed

It specifies which claim in the OAuth JWT token Snowflake should use to look up the Snowflake username; using 'sub' maps by the IdP's user subject, while 'upn' maps by the user's principal name — the choice determines how tokens map to Snowflake users and affects spoofability
It specifies the Snowflake column in ACCOUNT_USAGE.USERS that is matched against the OAuth token to find the user; valid values are LOGIN_NAME, EMAIL, or DISPLAY_NAME
It controls whether Snowflake validates the OAuth token signature; setting it to 'VERIFIED' enables signature verification and 'UNVERIFIED' skips it for trusted internal tokens

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because EXTERNAL_OAUTH_SNOWFLAKE_USER_MAPPING_ATTRIBUTE specifies which JWT claim Snowflake extracts from the OAuth token and then looks up in Snowflake's user database to identify the user. Common values include 'sub' (subject — the IdP's unique user identifier) and 'upn' (user principal name — typically the email-format identity). The security implication is significant: if mapped to a claim that an attacker can control or predict (e.g., a non-unique or guessable attribute), the mapping can be spoofed. Using a stable, unique, IdP-controlled claim like 'sub' is more secure than using a mutable claim like email.

**A** is incorrect — OAuth scope claims are controlled by a different parameter.

**C** is incorrect — the mapping is to a Snowflake user attribute (like LOGIN_NAME), not directly to ACCOUNT_USAGE columns.

**D** is incorrect — token signature validation is always performed and is not controlled by this parameter.

[External OAuth Overview | Snowflake Documentation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/oauth-ext-overview)

questions.stem: A development team needs to store credentials for connecting to an external REST API from a Snowpark Python UDF. The credentials should not appear in UDF code or be visible to other Snowflake users. Which Snowflake feature is designed for this purpose?

options.text: Store credentials as VARIANT values in an internal stage and reference them via a file URL in the UDF
Create a SECRET object using CREATE SECRET with the credential values; grant USAGE on the secret to the UDF's executing role; reference the secret in the UDF or integration definition
Use ACCOUNTADMIN to SET SESSION VARIABLE credentials = 'api_key_value'; UDFs can read session variables from their execution context
Embed credentials in the UDF code and encrypt them with AES-256; only users with ACCOUNTADMIN can decrypt and view the embedded credentials

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Snowflake's CREATE SECRET object is specifically designed for secure credential storage. Secrets store sensitive values (API keys, OAuth tokens, username/password pairs) in encrypted form within Snowflake. The secret's value is never visible in SHOW SECRETS output or when querying INFORMATION_SCHEMA — only the metadata is visible. UDFs and external network rules can reference secrets by name, and access is controlled through GRANT USAGE ON SECRET to specific roles. This provides credential management with Snowflake's native RBAC.

**A** is incorrect — storing credentials in a stage file exposes them to anyone with USAGE on the stage.

**C** is incorrect — session variables are visible in query profiles and session context; they are not a secure credential store.

**D** is incorrect — embedding encrypted credentials in UDF code is an anti-pattern; the encryption logic and keys would also need to be in the code.

[Monitor query activity with Query History](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/ui-query-profile)

questions.stem: An architect is designing a Tri-Secret Secure implementation. The security team asks about the consequences if the customer-managed key (CMK) is accidentally deleted from AWS KMS (not just disabled, but permanently deleted). Select TWO accurate statements about this scenario. (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowflake can recover the data if the customer contacts Snowflake Support within the 7-day Fail-Safe window
The data encrypted with the composite key is permanently and irrecoverably inaccessible; even Snowflake cannot decrypt the data without the CMK
AWS KMS enforces a mandatory 7-30 day pending deletion period before permanently deleting a CMK, providing a recovery window
Snowflake automatically detects the CMK deletion and re-encrypts the data with a new Snowflake-managed key during the pending deletion period
The Snowflake account becomes inaccessible immediately upon key deletion; all compute operations fail including warehouse start attempts

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Tri-Secret Secure creates a composite encryption key that requires both Snowflake's key AND the customer's CMK to decrypt data. If the CMK is permanently deleted, the composite key can never be reconstructed, making the data permanently inaccessible. Not even Snowflake can recover it. This is the intended 'kill switch' behavior, but accidental permanent deletion is catastrophic.

**C** is correct and provides the critical safety mechanism: AWS KMS enforces a mandatory pending deletion period of 7 to 30 days (configurable by the customer) before a CMK is permanently deleted. During this window, the customer can cancel the deletion and restore access. This is why AWS recommends scheduling deletion with the maximum wait period and treating CMK deletion as a high-risk operation.

**A** is incorrect — Snowflake Support cannot recover data if the CMK is gone; Fail-Safe stores encrypted data, and without the CMK, even Fail-Safe data is unreadable.

**D** is incorrect — Snowflake does not detect CMK deletion and re-encrypt; the system simply fails when it tries to use the deleted key.

**E** is correct as a behavior description but was not listed as one of the two correct answers — it overlaps with B.

[Understanding and viewing Fail-safe](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-failsafe)

questions.stem: A company has a mixed Snowflake workload: nightly ETL loads (8 PM–2 AM), BI dashboard queries (8 AM–6 PM), and ad-hoc data science queries that run unpredictably throughout the day. An architect is designing the warehouse credit allocation strategy. Which design best balances performance and cost?

options.text: Create one large XLarge warehouse for all three workload types; this avoids idle warehouses and maximizes cache reuse across workloads

Create three dedicated warehouses: a Large for ETL (scheduled, suspends at 2 AM), a multi-cluster Medium for BI (MIN=1, MAX=3, Standard scaling, suspends at 6 PM), and an XLarge on-demand for data science (high AUTO_SUSPEND = 300s)
Create a single 4XLarge multi-cluster warehouse with MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT=10 and ECONOMY scaling to handle all three workloads; let Snowflake manage cluster allocation automatically
Use only serverless compute (Snowpipe Streaming, Serverless Tasks) for all three workloads to avoid warehouse credit consumption

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because workload isolation is a core Snowflake cost optimization principle — different workloads have different performance profiles, concurrency needs, and schedules. Dedicated warehouses prevent ETL from competing with BI queries for compute resources (and vice versa). The ETL warehouse is scheduled and sized for batch throughput (Large, not multi-cluster). The BI warehouse handles concurrency with multi-cluster Standard scaling. The data science warehouse is on-demand and auto-suspends quickly when idle. This right-sizing approach minimizes idle cost while ensuring each workload gets appropriate resources. A puts all workloads on one warehouse, creating queuing and contention. C uses a single massive warehouse with Economy scaling — Economy allows queuing, unacceptable for BI SLAs, and a 4XLarge is over-provisioned for short ETL loads.

**D** is incorrect — not all workloads can use serverless compute; standard SQL queries require warehouses.

[Multi-cluster warehouses](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/warehouses-multicluster)

questions.stem: An architect queries ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY and notices that a specific warehouse consistently uses 40-50 credits per day Monday through Thursday, drops to 5 credits on Friday, and uses 0 credits on weekends. A new project proposal will add 30 credits/day of warehouse usage starting next month. Which analysis should the architect perform to determine if existing warehouse capacity can absorb this, and what does METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provide for this analysis?

options.text: METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides real-time credit consumption by warehouse by day, including peak concurrency metrics; the architect should check if peak concurrency leaves room for additional workloads

METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily credit totals by warehouse (CREDITS_USED, CREDITS_USED_COMPUTE, CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES); the architect should analyze the historical trend to forecast whether a 30-credit/day increase fits within budget and whether the warehouse utilization pattern indicates headroom or saturation
METERING_DAILY_HISTORY does not break down by warehouse; the architect must use ACCOUNT_USAGE.WAREHOUSE_METERING_HISTORY for per-warehouse data
METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides billing currency amounts only; credit counts require querying ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_HISTORY instead

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because ACCOUNT_USAGE.METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily credit consumption aggregated by service type (including warehouses) with fields such as CREDITS_USED, CREDITS_USED_COMPUTE, and CREDITS_USED_CLOUD_SERVICES. This view is ideal for trend analysis — the architect can see the weekly pattern (40-50 credits weekdays, 0 weekends), project the impact of 30 additional credits, and assess whether budget thresholds will be breached. For capacity analysis, the architect also needs to understand warehouse queuing metrics (from WAREHOUSE_EVENTS_HISTORY) to determine if additional workload would cause queuing.

**A** is incorrect — METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides daily totals, not real-time concurrency metrics.

**C** is incorrect — METERING_DAILY_HISTORY does include per-service breakdowns including warehouse credits.

**D** is incorrect — METERING_DAILY_HISTORY provides both credit counts and can be joined to currency data; it is not limited to currency amounts.

questions.stem: A 5 TB fact table is clustered on SALE_DATE. An architect runs SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION and observes average_depth = 1.2 and average_overlaps = 0.3, indicating excellent clustering. However, queries that filter on both SALE_DATE and PRODUCT_CATEGORY (10,000 distinct values) still read more micro-partitions than expected. What is the most likely explanation, and what should the architect do?

options.text: The existing clustering on SALE_DATE is failing; drop and recreate with a higher-cardinality key

The single-column clustering key on SALE_DATE does not co-locate rows by PRODUCT_CATEGORY within each date range; adding PRODUCT_CATEGORY as a second key in a composite clustering key will further reduce micro-partitions scanned for queries filtering on both columns
Enable the Search Optimization Service on SALE_DATE to supplement the clustering key for range predicates
Increase the warehouse size so the query engine can scan more micro-partitions in parallel, reducing wall-clock time

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because a single-column clustering key on SALE_DATE organises micro-partitions by date but does not control how rows are sorted within a date's micro-partitions by PRODUCT_CATEGORY. A composite clustering key of (SALE_DATE, PRODUCT_CATEGORY) would further group rows by product within each date range, enabling pruning on both predicates simultaneously and reducing the number of micro-partitions read. The excellent average_depth for SALE_DATE already achieves good pruning on the date filter, but the additional PRODUCT_CATEGORY filter still requires scanning all partitions for that date.

**A** is wrong — the clustering is working well for the leading key; the issue is that a second column is not part of the key.

**C** is incorrect — SOS targets equality predicates on high-cardinality columns for point lookups, but SALE_DATE is a range predicate and PRODUCT_CATEGORY is not a point lookup problem here. D adds compute but does not reduce the number of micro-partitions scanned.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)

questions.stem: A data governance team wants to implement a tag-based masking policy so that any column tagged with 'SENSITIVITY' = 'PII' is automatically masked for non-privileged users without requiring individual column-level policy assignments. Select TWO accurate statements about this tag-based masking policy approach. (Select TWO)

options.text: A tag-based masking policy is created using CREATE MASKING POLICY and then attached to a TAG object using ALTER TAG ... SET MASKING POLICY, causing the policy to apply automatically to all columns bearing that tag
Tag-based masking policies require the column's data type to match the masking policy signature; a VARCHAR masking policy will not apply to a NUMBER column even if both carry the same tag
ACCOUNT_USAGE.POLICY_REFERENCES can be queried to verify which columns are protected by tag-based masking policies, providing governance evidence
A tag-based masking policy and a column-level masking policy applied to the same column do not conflict; both are enforced simultaneously and the more restrictive result is returned

Snowflake Data Classification can automatically assign system-defined tags (SEMANTIC_CATEGORY, PRIVACY_CATEGORY) to columns; tag-based masking policies can then act on these system tags to auto-protect classified PII columns

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because this is the exact DDL pattern for tag-based masking: create a masking policy with the desired logic, then attach it to a TAG with ALTER TAG my_tag SET MASKING POLICY my_mask. All columns tagged with my_tag automatically inherit the masking policy without per-column ALTER TABLE commands.

**B** is correct and is an important constraint: the masking policy must have a signature (data type) compatible with the tagged column. A VARCHAR masking policy (that takes a VARCHAR argument) will not be applied to a NUMBER column — Snowflake requires type matching. Governance teams must register separate policies for each data type that PII columns can have.

**C** is accurate (POLICY_REFERENCES does show tag-based coverage) but the question asks for statements about the tag-based masking approach, and C describes a query view rather than the approach mechanics.

**D** is incorrect — if both a tag-based policy and a direct column-level policy exist, the column-level policy takes precedence; they are not both applied simultaneously.

**E** is accurate about Data Classification, but it describes an adjacent feature rather than how tag-based masking policies work.

[Understanding Column-level Security](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/security-column-intro)

questions.stem: A company has a 10 TB orders table accessed by two distinct query patterns: (1) nightly ETL aggregations that scan the full table grouped by ORDER_DATE ranges, and (2) intraday operational queries that look up single orders by ORDER_ID (UUID). An architect must choose between enabling Automatic Clustering on ORDER_DATE vs. enabling Search Optimization Service on ORDER_ID. Which design decision is correct?

options.text: Use Automatic Clustering on ORDER_DATE only; the ETL aggregations benefit from pruning and the UUID lookups will also benefit because clustered partitions are smaller
Use Search Optimization Service on ORDER_ID only; SOS handles both point lookups and range scans equally well

Implement both independently: Automatic Clustering on ORDER_DATE for the ETL date-range aggregations and Search Optimization Service on ORDER_ID (column-level) for the UUID point lookups — each optimisation targets a different access pattern
Neither is needed; increasing the warehouse size to 4XLarge will make both query patterns fast enough by parallelising the full-table scans

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Automatic Clustering and Search Optimization Service are complementary, not mutually exclusive, optimisations that address different query patterns on the same table. Clustering on ORDER_DATE organises micro-partitions so that ETL queries filtering by date ranges can prune the vast majority of the table. SOS on ORDER_ID builds bloom-filter access paths that allow UUID equality lookups to skip irrelevant micro-partitions — without SOS, a UUID lookup on an unclustered column in a 10 TB table scans all partitions. Column-level SOS (ON EQUALITY(ORDER_ID)) limits the SOS cost to only the ORDER_ID column rather than indexing the entire table.

**A** is partially correct for the ETL workload but clustering on ORDER_DATE does not help UUID lookups — ORDER_ID values are randomly distributed across all partitions regardless of date ordering.

**B** is incorrect — SOS does not benefit range scans or aggregations; it is specifically for equality predicates. D ignores the fundamental I/O bottleneck; more compute nodes scan more partitions in parallel but the number of partitions scanned does not decrease.

[Understanding Snowflake Table Structures](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/tables-micro-partitions)
